reason: The item cleanly targets the risk-vs-severity (hazard) distinction and elicits boundary-aware engineering judgments (e.g., earlier BMS intervention vs. downstream consequence magnitude) rather than rote recall. Decoys are mostly single-fault and plausible (conflation, over-inference from a proxy, and unjustified convergence at high rates), though there is some residual ambiguity around whether “hazard” is being interpreted purely as severity or as an application-weighted risk metric, slightly softening discriminative sharpness.

The item cleanly targets admissible inference vs overreach from SOC-dependent ARC observables and from a model trained on a specific cell format/chemistry, with strong boundary pressure around transferability and universal-safety claims. Decoys are mostly single-fault and plausible, though two options (B, F) share the same overgeneralization pattern and option A’s wording risks a subtle proxy jump (pressure to total gas). Overall it should separate experts who respect scope/causality limits from those who extrapolate trends into unconditional guarantees or unvalidated model transfer.

The item tightly targets a single mechanistic judgment: rejecting an unjustified single-factor attribution by using anode SEI compositional evidence as a competing causal contributor to self-heating onset. It has strong discriminative power because it forces boundary-aware reasoning about what SEI intermediate species imply (ongoing reactive/unstable interphase) and why that undermines “entirely due to cathode stabilization,” while still preserving a role for Fe dissolution suppression. Minor residual ambiguity remains in how much explanatory weight to assign each mechanism, but the prompt and must-include constraints largely prevent overclaiming.
